skills.name: Variables
skills.desc: Variables are used to store data in C++. They have a specific data type and can hold values that can be changed during program execution.

skills.name: Data Types
skills.desc: Data types define the type of data that a variable can hold, such as int, double, char, etc., and determine how the data is stored and manipulated.

skills.name: Operators
skills.desc: Operators in C++ are used for performing various operations on data, such as arithmetic, logical, and relational operations.

skills.name: Booleans
skills.desc: Booleans represent true or false values and are used for making logical decisions in C++ programs.

skills.name: Arrays
skills.desc: Arrays are collections of elements of the same data type, allowing you to store and manipulate multiple values under a single variable name.

skills.name: Strings
skills.desc: Strings are sequences of characters used for working with text data in C++, and they have built-in functions for text manipulation.

skills.name: Conditions
skills.desc: Conditions in C++ are used to make decisions in the program flow, allowing different actions to be taken based on specified conditions.

skills.name: If...Else
skills.desc: The if...else statement is used to execute different code blocks based on whether a specified condition is true or false.

skills.name: Switch
skills.desc: The switch statement allows you to select one of many code blocks to be executed, based on the value of a given expression.

skills.name: For Loop
skills.desc: The for loop is used for iterating over a range of values or elements, executing a set of statements a specified number of times.

skills.name: While Loop
skills.desc: The while loop repeats a set of statements as long as a specified condition is true, allowing for flexible looping.

skills.name: Break/Continue
skills.desc: The break statement is used to exit a loop prematurely, while the continue statement skips the rest of the current iteration and proceeds to the next.

skills.name: Function Parameters
skills.desc: Function parameters are variables used to pass data to a function, allowing the function to work with different input values.

skills.name: Function Overloading
skills.desc: Function overloading enables the definition of multiple functions with the same name but different parameter lists, improving code readability.

skills.name: Recursion
skills.desc: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself, often used for solving problems that can be broken down into smaller, similar subproblems.

skills.name: Classes/Objects
skills.desc: Classes serve as blueprints for creating objects, while objects are instances of classes that encapsulate data and behavior.

skills.name: Class Methods
skills.desc: Class methods are functions defined within a class and are used to perform operations on class attributes.

skills.name: Access Specifiers
skills.desc: Access specifiers (public, private, protected) determine the visibility and access level of class members in C++.

skills.name: Constructors
skills.desc: Constructors are special member functions used to initialize class objects, providing a way to set initial values.

skills.name: Encapsulation
skills.desc: Encapsulation is the concept of bundling data and methods that operate on that data into a single unit, known as a class.

skills.name: Inheritance
skills.desc: Inheritance allows a class to inherit properties and methods from another class, promoting code reusability.

skills.name: Polymorphism
skills.desc: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated as objects of a common base class, facilitating code flexibility and extensibility.

skills.name: Pointers
skills.desc: Pointers are variables that store memory addresses, enabling dynamic memory allocation and manipulation.

skills.name: References
skills.desc: References provide an alternative way to alias variables, offering a level of indirection without the complexity of pointers.

skills.name: Structures
skills.desc: Structures allow you to create user-defined data types that can contain members of different data types.

skills.name: Exceptions
skills.desc: Exceptions are used for handling runtime errors and exceptional situations, improving program robustness.

skills.name: Templates
skills.desc: Templates are used for generic programming, allowing the creation of functions and classes that work with multiple data types.

skills.name: Output
skills.desc: Output in C++ refers to displaying data or results to the console or other output streams, typically using cout.

skills.name: User Input
skills.desc: User input is the process of accepting data or values from the user during program execution, often using cin.

skills.name: Files
skills.desc: File handling involves reading from and writing to external files, allowing data to be stored persistently.

skills.name: Math
skills.desc: The Standard Library includes mathematical functions and constants for common mathematical operations.

skills.name: String Manipulation
skills.desc: String manipulation functions in the Standard Library offer a variety of tools for working with strings.

skills.name: Standard Containers
skills.desc: Standard containers such as vectors, lists, and maps provide data structures for storing and manipulating collections of data.

skills.name: Comments
skills.desc: Comments are used to provide explanatory notes within the code, enhancing code readability and understanding.

skills.name: Code Formatting
skills.desc: Code formatting involves following a consistent style and indentation to improve code readability and maintainability.
